solver_view.stem: An LFP battery is subjected to oven heating. Voltage remains stable at ~3.33 V before venting. Expansion force anomaly (F > 1300 N) is detectable before venting. Thermal runaway is defined as maximum temperature rise rate exceeding 1°C/s. At 180°C oven temperature, the interval from force anomaly to TR onset is ~10,318 s. At 250°C it is ~3,758 s. Which statements about this early-warning system are supported?
options: A. Voltage signal provides an earlier warning than expansion force signal across all tested oven temperatures.
B. The expansion force anomaly signal consistently appears before voltage and surface temperature anomalies regardless of oven temperature.
C. The lead time provided by expansion force warning decreases as oven temperature increases in TR scenarios.
D. At 180°C oven temperature, expansion force monitoring can provide approximately 3 hours of advance warning before TR onset.
E. The early-warning advantage of expansion force monitoring is greater at lower oven temperatures than at temperatures ≥180°C.
F. Because voltage is stable before venting at all tested temperatures, voltage monitoring alone is sufficient for TR early warning.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states expansion force consistently emerges as one of the initial abnormal signals, appearing before voltage anomalies.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Expansion force consistently emerges as one of the initial abnormal signals detected, irrespective of oven temperature.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Lead time for expansion force warning decreases with increasing oven temperature in TR scenarios, per direct evidence.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: At 180°C, time span from force anomaly to TR onset is 10,318 s, explicitly stated to allow ~3 h advance warning.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states expansion force early warning is more effective under lower oven temperature scenarios than at ≥180°C.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Stable voltage before venting means voltage lacks discriminating power; expansion force is the earlier and more informative signal.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Relative warning lead time of expansion force vs. voltage signals, and how oven temperature modulates warning effectiveness
analysis.primary_boundary: Evidence is restricted to LFP batteries under oven heating; voltage stability before venting does not imply voltage is a useful early warning signal
intended_error_modes: causal overclaim (A: voltage earlier than force)
disallowed_extrapolations: Extending lead-time advantage of expansion force to all oven temperatures equally
Claiming voltage drop is an early TR indicator in this regime

solver_view.stem: A battery module of LiNi0.8Co0.15Al0.05O2 cells operates under 3C discharge rate. SEI decomposition initiates at 74°C; self-heating commences at 145°C; TR triggers at 166°C. A gaseous fire extinguishing agent is deployed at the onset of flames. The module uses nitrogen gas cooling at 1 m/s flow and 2 bar ambient pressure. Which conclusions are supported by the evidence?
options: A. SEI decomposition at 74°C immediately triggers self-heating and thermal runaway in the cell.
B. Gaseous extinguishing agents reliably prevent thermal runaway propagation to adjacent cells by eliminating the heat source.
C. Nitrogen provides better propagation suppression than argon under equivalent flow conditions because nitrogen has higher heat capacity.
D. At 3C discharge rate, operating at ≥1 m/s flow and ≥2 bar pressure is identified as a minimum condition to effectively mitigate flame propagation.
E. Increasing ambient pressure from 1 bar to 3 bar completely inhibits thermal runaway propagation under 3C discharge and 0.5 m/s flow conditions.
F. Argon outperforms nitrogen in cooling effectiveness because argon has higher density than nitrogen.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: SEI decomposition at 74°C is the first exothermic reaction, but self-heating only commences at 145°C; immediate TR is not supported.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Gaseous agents extinguish flames but fail to significantly lower cell temperatures; exothermic reactions persist, enabling re-ignition or propagation.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Nitrogen's superior suppression over argon is attributed to higher heat capacity; argon's higher density does not compensate for its lower heat capacity.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states 3C discharge requires at least 1 m/s flow and 2 bar pressure to effectively mitigate flame propagation.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Under 3C discharge and 0.5 m/s flow, propagation was completely inhibited at 3 bar, with slowing observed at 2 bar.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Argon's higher density is explicitly overridden by its lower heat capacity, making it the least effective cooling gas tested.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Distinguishing SEI decomposition as TR onset trigger vs. sustained self-heating; gaseous agent limitations; gas property ranking for cooling
analysis.primary_boundary: Pressure suppression result is specific to 3C discharge and 0.5 m/s flow; gaseous agent limitation applies post-extinguishment only
intended_error_modes: causal overclaim (A: SEI decomposition = immediate TR)
boundary overreach (B: gaseous agent prevents propagation)
mechanism substitution (F: density dominates over heat capacity)
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming complete inhibition at 3 bar applies to all discharge rates
Claiming gaseous agents are ineffective at extinguishing flames

solver_view.stem: A fleet safety analyst reviews BMS data from 41 electric vehicles involved in thermal runaway accidents. Vehicles are classified as latent-failure (anomalous data >1 week before accident) or sudden-failure (no prior anomaly). Internal short circuit (ISC) is the most common sudden-failure type. An ISC leading to TR typically produces a voltage drop. One vehicle showed a voltage spike to 17 V just before the accident. Which conclusions are supported?
options: A. The voltage spike to 17 V in the anomalous vehicle is most likely caused by an internal short circuit triggering thermal runaway.
B. Among 41 accidental vehicles, sudden-failure cases outnumber latent-failure cases.
C. For sudden-failure vehicles, the Abnormal Rate Frequency Distribution curve alone is sufficient to determine the root cause of failure.
D. The voltage spike to 17 V is inconsistent with ISC-induced TR and is more likely attributable to BMS malfunction or data transmission error from mechanical impact.
E. Continuous low voltage in a cell can lead to lithium deposition, internal short circuit, and ultimately thermal runaway.
F. A voltage difference of 0.025 V between cells is guaranteed to trigger BMS alarm systems in commercial vehicles.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: ISC-induced TR causes voltage drop, not spike; the 17 V spike is attributed to BMS malfunction or mechanical damage to data wires.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The latent-to-sudden ratio is 17:24, confirming sudden-failure cases outnumber latent-failure cases in this dataset.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states ARFD alone is insufficient for sudden-failure vehicles; full status data from the prior week is required.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: A voltage spike contradicts the ISC-TR voltage-drop signature; simultaneous mechanical damage to battery and data wires is the supported explanation.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Continuous low voltage leads to lithium deposition on the anode, causing ISC and redox reactions with electrolyte, triggering TR.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows a 0.025 V difference in vehicle 8 did not trigger the alarm system, directly contradicting this claim.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Distinguishing ISC voltage signature from mechanical-damage artifacts; BMS alarm threshold limitations; ARFD sufficiency boundary
analysis.primary_boundary: Voltage spike ≠ ISC signature; 0.025 V difference insufficient to trigger alarm in observed case; ARFD insufficient for sudden-failure root cause
intended_error_modes: mechanism substitution (A: spike attributed to ISC)
boundary overreach (C: ARFD sufficient; F: 0.025 V always triggers alarm)
disallowed_extrapolations: Generalizing that any voltage anomaly indicates ISC
Claiming ARFD is sufficient for latent-failure analysis

solver_view.prompt: An LFP battery module is monitored using three signal channels: surface temperature, voltage, and expansion force. The module is exposed to oven heating at temperatures ranging from 150°C to 250°C. Thermal runaway is defined as maximum temperature rise rate exceeding 1°C/s, and ISC events occur only when oven temperature exceeds 180°C. The ISC trigger temperature (back surface) remains between 228°C and 235°C regardless of oven temperature. Explain why voltage monitoring alone is insufficient as an early warning strategy in this scenario, and identify which signal provides the earliest warning and how its effectiveness changes with oven temperature.

ground_truth.gold_answer: Voltage monitoring alone is insufficient because voltage remains relatively stable at approximately 3.33 V before venting across all tested oven temperatures, providing no discriminating early-warning signal prior to the critical event. Expansion force is the signal that consistently appears earliest among the three channels, irrespective of oven temperature, with anomaly detection (F > 1300 N) preceding both voltage and surface temperature anomalies. However, the effectiveness of expansion force warning decreases as oven temperature increases: at 180°C the lead time from force anomaly to TR onset is approximately 10,318 s (~3 hours), whereas at 250°C it shrinks to approximately 3,758 s. Therefore, expansion force monitoring is most advantageous at lower oven temperatures, and its warning advantage diminishes at higher oven temperatures where TR progression is faster.
must_include: voltage remains stable (~3.33 V) before venting, making it uninformative as an early warning
expansion force anomaly signal appears earliest regardless of oven temperature
lead time decreases as oven temperature increases in TR scenarios
at 180°C lead time is approximately 10,318 s; at 250°C it is approximately 3,758 s
expansion force warning is more effective at lower oven temperatures than at temperatures ≥180°C
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming voltage drop is an early warning indicator in this LFP oven-heating regime
Claiming expansion force warning lead time is constant across oven temperatures
Asserting that ISC trigger temperature varies with oven temperature
Extending conclusions to non-LFP chemistries or non-oven-heating abuse modes

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Multi-signal early warning comparison: voltage stability as a limitation, expansion force as the earliest indicator, and the non-monotonic (decreasing) lead time with increasing oven temperature
analysis.primary_boundary: Conclusions restricted to LFP batteries under oven heating at 150–250°C; voltage stability finding applies before venting only; ISC trigger temperature is oven-temperature-independent within tested range
intended_error_modes: Asserting voltage is a useful early warning signal because it eventually drops at TR onset
Claiming expansion force warning effectiveness is uniform across all oven temperatures
Confusing ISC trigger temperature (oven-independent) with TR trigger temperature (oven-dependent)
